events.title: Virtual Author Talk: Paula J. Johnson
events.description: Join us for a heartwarming online conversation with Smithsonian curator Paula J. Johnson as she explores the legacy of culinary icon Julia Child, inspired by her book Julia Child’s Kitchen: The Design, Tools, Stories, and Legacy of an Iconic Space. Featuring interviews with chefs who knew Julia, insights into her favorite tools, and stunning photographs, the book offers an intimate look at the kitchen that shaped a generation of home cooks. On display at the National Museum of American History for over two decades, Julia’s 20’ x 14’ kitchen is a beloved destination filled with more than a thousand items that reflect her vibrant personality and pioneering spirit. This conversation is a must for anyone who loves cooking, history, or Julia herself. --- The free, virtual series is open to the public and takes place two to three times a month. All author talks will include the opportunity to ask the author questions. Click here for a complete list of upcoming events in this series. A link for this event will be sent approximately 24 hours before the event. Participants who register within 24 hours of the event will receive a single email containing the link to the program. This program will be recorded and posted here after the event and will only be available for viewing for a limited time. --- The Santa Clara County Library District is proud to present a virtual monthly author talk series featuring bestselling, award-winning, and highly acclaimed authors from around the world. The featured writers and thought leaders will cover a wide range of fiction and nonfiction genres.---For accessibility accommodations (ex. ASL), please fill out the online form at sccld.org/accessibility 7+days prior to the event. If you need assistance with the form, speak with a staff member at your local library, call (408) 540-3945, or email us at ask@lib.sccgov.org.

Study Zone Study Zone programs provide volunteer-led academic support for grades K-12. Programs include Tutors, Math Club, Reading Buddies and Kids & Teens Talk Time. Available online and in libraries. Online Study Zone programs are supervised by library staff. Visit Study Zone for the Terms of Use Agreement and steps KCLS is taking to maintain a safe and positive online space. Reasonable accommodation for people with disabilities is available by request. Email access@kcls.org at least seven days before the event. Automated closed captioning is always available for online events.

events.title: Under the Dundee Sun Book Club
events.description: Each year, Omaha Public Library encourages the community to join together in reading one book as part of Omaha Reads. This year we invite you to read "Remarkably Bright Creatures” by Shelby Van Pelt. Join us this month for a lively discussion of the Omaha Reads title. OPL Book Club discussions are led by OPL staff and are open to everyone. A luminous debut novel about a widow's unlikely friendship with a giant Pacific octopus reluctantly residing at the local aquarium-and the truths she finally uncovers about her son's disappearance 30 years ago. Omaha Reads promotes literacy and provides the City with a common theme to discuss. One book is selected through a public vote and promoted through book talks, author visits, and related programs.Copies of “Remarkably Bright Creatures” can be reserved for checkout from the OPL catalog. All Omaha Public Library locations are wheelchair accessible. Need sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please email Nancy Novotny at NNovotny@omahalibrary.org to request accommodation. Requests must be made at least five business days before the activity or event.

events.title: Summer Book Bingo & Book Swap
events.description: Hey adults and teens! Do you miss the days of reading (and winning prizes) all summer long? We’ve got just the club for you! Summer Book Bingo is here to provide summer fun for adults and teens who want to participate in a summer reading club. How to Participate Visit the Library service desk to pick up your Bingo card, or stop by the Book Bingo & Book Swap Booth every Tuesday evening throughout July and August. Receive a small prize when you sign up and additional rewards when you report your progress. Bring a book to swap and connect with fellow readers. Submit your completed Bingo card with your name and contact information Grand Prize Draw Complete 20 squares or more on your Bingo card and you'll be entered to win one of three grand prizes: A Library tote bag filled with lots of book goodies A DIY Miniature House kit A bread maker The grand prize draw will be in September.

events.title: Summer Book Bingo Lemonade Social
events.description: Hey adults and teens, do you miss the days of reading (and winning prizes) all summer long? We’ve got just the club for you! Summer Book Bingo is here to provide summer fun for teens and adults who want to participate in a summer reading club.How do I participate? Visit an Information Desk to collect your bingo card. Then, get reading to complete our cheeky challenges. Stop by a Lemonade Social to report on your progress, collect your mini-prizes, drink some lemonade, and socialize. If you fill up 20 squares on your bingo card, you'll be entered to win one of our grand prizes!

January-March: The Winner Takes It All (Recent Oscar Award winning movies) January: The Holdovers – Rated R February: Wicked – Rated PG March: Conclave – Rated PG Craft Kit: Small Clay Trophy Painting Kits April-June: So Bad It’s Good (Bad movies that are fun to watch) April: Troll 2 (1990) – Rated PG-13 May: Moonfall – Rated PG-13 June: Twilight – Rated PG-13 Craft Kit: Moon Puzzle July-September: All Around the World (Movies from other countries with English subtitles) July: Train to Busan (Korean) - Not Rated August: Fear (Bulgarian) - Not Rated September: The Sea Inside (Spanish) - Rated PG-13 Craft Kit: Scratch Art October-December: Everybody Loves a Classic (Classic movies from different genres) October: Suspiria (1977) – Not Rated November: Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring - Rated PG-13 December: Clue (1985) – Rated PG Craft Kit: Fall and Winter Globes

events.title: Book Clubs of EPL: Midnight in Chernobyl : the untold story of the world's greatest nuclear disaster
events.description: Love reading? Love to talk about books you've read? Come join us for lively discussions on all types of fiction and non-fiction. We'll feature different titles and authors each month. Come and share your impressions, thoughts and interpretations of a book we have all read.The Book we will be reading this month will be "Midnight in Chernobyl : the untold story of the world's greatest nuclear disaster"Draws on twenty years of research, recently declassified files, and interviews with survivors in an account of the 1986 Chernobyl nuclear power plant disaster that also reveals how propaganda and secrets have created additional dangers

events.title: Sounds from the Valley: Music and Poetry
events.description: Head down to Edmonton's Snow Valley Ski Club to take in a FREE concert with music, poetry and art! Listen to artists from EPL's Capital City Records and Capital City Press as they share their work at the bottom of the ski hill in the beautiful Rainbow Valley. Free of charge, we'll be collecting non-perishable food items for the Edmonton Food Bank.NOTE: Due to the very high popularity of the event this year, we suggest those who are able to bring their own lawn chairs to do so to ensure ample seating for everyone. If you don’t have a lawn chair, that is totally okay – we can absolutely make it work! Music from Capital City Records Artist, Lindsey Walker Lindsey Walker is an award-winning Edmonton-based powerhouse musician best known for her commanding vocal presence and vulnerable charming stage banter. Lindsey’s haunting yet arousing vocals draw on the doom-pop sensibilities of Kimbra; the rocking charisma of Heart; and the meticulous orchestration of St. Vincent. With the award-winning album, “this desolate bliss.” under her belt, Lindsey’s latest release, “Something Real”, is a trio of moody, anthemic tracks that’ll make you want to grab your lover and run full-tilt into the darkness. Whether Lindsey is performing solo with her electric guitar, as a duo with her good friend on Keys, or with her full band of misfits, you will always get the essence of Lindsey Walker - soulful, charming and deeply heartfelt. When she’s not wooing audiences with her live performance, Lindsey is a sound designer and composer with multiple theatre credits to her name, working for many established theatre companies in Alberta. Lindsey’s also a very, very funny lady who entertains audiences as a founding member of the sketch comedy troupe Sketchy Broads and a principal player on the improv soap opera Die-Nasty!. Whether Lindsey is performing solo with her electric guitar, as a duo with her good friend on Keys, or with her full band of misfits, you will always get the essence of Lindsey Walker - soulful, charming and deeply heartfelt. Readings from Capital City Press Authors: Books by Katherine Koller are Voices of the Land: The Seed Savers and Other Plays, Art Lessons, a novel, and two story collections, Winning Chance and Earthen. Her poetry has appeared in Prairie Journal, NorthWord, Happiness Reflected, Funicular, The Lupine Review, Fire from the Heart, The Amphibian, and online at Poetry Pause. Katherine's latest play, an adaptation of Jane Austen’s Persuasion, was developed in the Citadel Playwrights Lab and will be presented at Concordia University Edmonton in November 2026. Grace Bly is a psychology student at the University of Alberta who frequently writes creative nonfiction and poetry. In her free time, she enjoys crocheting, drawing, reading, and buying more books than she has time to read.

Come together with your fellow book lovers and discover your next literary adventure at our monthly Book Club, facilitated and led by Angie McGowan. Everyone is welcome to join to share about their current reads or just to hang out and chat about books in general. Every 1st Tuesday of the month @ 6PM Get accessibility information.

book.title: lovers and discover your next literary adventure at our monthly Book Club, facilitated and led
book.author: Angie McGowan.

events.title: Mystery Book Club
events.description: Meet with your neighbors and fellow mystery lovers on the first Tuesday night of each month for a lively discussion of classic and current mysteries. Read this month's selection, drop in and join the fun! For August 4 the club will be discussing The Paris Widow by Kimberly Belle SynopsisWhen Stella met Adam, she thought she had finally found a nice, normal guy--a welcome change from her previous boyfriend and her precarious jetsetter lifestyle with him. But her secure world comes crashing down when Adam goes missing after an explosion in the city square. Unable to reach him, she panics. As the French police investigate, it's revealed that Adam was on their radar as a dealer of rare and stolen antiquities with a long roster of criminal clients. Reeling from this news, Stella is determined not to leave Paris until she has the full story. Was Adam a random victim or the target of the explosion? And why is someone following her through the streets of Paris? --Provided by the publisher The Paris Widow Library staff may take photos, videos, or audio recordings at our events. Please tell us if you do not wish to be photographed, video-recorded, or audio-recorded. We will always respect your requests! An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter or closed captioning can be provided for this program if requested at least seven (7) days in advance. For assistance, please contact your library or accessibility@aclibrary.org.

events.title: One Village, One Book
events.description: Our book club meets monthly at the library to discuss books set in Oakland and the Bay Area or by Bay Area authors. We select our books from a variety of genres and eras so you’re sure to find something you’re interested in. We’ll get to learn more about our city, meet some neighbors and discuss what we’ve read. This year we’re mostly alternating fiction and non-fiction books. Meetings are the first Tuesday of the month (note the change from 2025!) from 6:30-7:30pm. Come to one discussion, a few or all of them! No pre-registration or commitment is required – all we ask is that you’ve read at least some of the book and are ready to discuss it. Here’s our 2026 schedule: (Quoted descriptions are from the library catalog unless otherwise noted.) January 6, 2026: Sourdough by Robin Sloan (fiction)We’re kicking off the year with a quick read about the very Bay Area intersection of tech and food culture. Lois is an exhausted software engineer who is gifted a very special sourdough starter by her favorite bakers when they must flee the country. Pretty soon she’s part of a mysterious underground food scene at the former Alameda Naval Air Station. February 3, 2026: Family Style: Memories of An American From Vietnam by Thien Pham (graphic memoir)The book that got the most votes from our members on our year-end poll! “Told through the lens of meaningful food and meals, this graphic novel chronicles the author’s childhood immigration to America, where food takes on new meaning as he and his family search for belonging, for happiness and for the American dream.” Pham is a graphic novelist, comic artist and educator based in Oakland. March 3, 2026: Deep Care: The Radical Activists Who Provided Abortions, Defied the Law, and Fought to Keep Clinics Open by Angela Hume (non-fiction)“The story of the radical feminist networks who worked outside the law to defend abortion. Deep Care follows generations of activists and clinicians who orbited the Women’s Choice clinic in Oakland from the early 1970s until 2010, as they worked underground and above ground, in small cells and broad coalitions and across political movements with grit, conviction, and allegiances of great trust to do what they believed needed to be done–despite the law, when required.” April 7, 2026: Moonglow by Michael Chabon (fiction)“A man bears witness to his grandfather’s deathbed confessions, which reveal his family’s long-buried history and his involvement in a mail-order novelty company, World War II and the space program.” Most of it isn’t set in Oakland, but it is the only book on this list that specifically mentions Montclair Library! May 5, 2026: Automatic Noodle by Annalee Newitz (science fiction)Our May read is a slim 163 pages, but packed with interesting ideas. This book is super popular as of December 2025, so you might want to get your hold in early. “In this semicozy tale set in a dystopian near future, robots open their own restaurant, build an unusual found family and achieve personal growth. A few years after the war that led to California’s secession from the United States, four robots awake in the flooded San Francisco takeout place where they were contracted to work.” (Kirkus) “Emboldened by newly granted civil rights from the California government, the bots reboot their lives in classic Bay Area form: by opening a noodle shop…In many ways, Newitz’s near-future San Francisco resembles today’s city, with not-so-subtle nods to AI anxieties, queer identity and the modern-day immigrant experience. It is, in the words of KQED’s Alexis Madrigal, ‘The most San Francisco book that has ever been or could ever have been written.’” (Leah Worthington) (Ebook listed separately) June 2, 2026: Moneyball by Michael Lewis (non-fiction)Baseball season is in full swing, which makes this the perfect time to read Michael Lewis’s nuanced exploration of “how Billy Beene, the general manager of the Oakland Athletics, used a new kind of thinking to build a successful and winning baseball team without spending enormous sums of money.” July 7, 2026: Girls Girls Girls by Shoshana von Blanckensee (fiction)In the summer of 1996, best friends (and secret girlfriends), Hannah and Sam drive from “New York, to the fabled queer paradise of San Francisco, free from the harsh gazes of their neighbors and the stifling demands of Hannah’s devout Orthodox Jewish mother. In San Francisco, they will finally be together as a real couple, out in the open, around other queer people.” But soon they’re working at a strip club, meeting new people and trying to figure out who just they are. August 4, 2026: Power Hungry: Women of the Black Panther Party and Freedom Summer and Their Fight to Feed a Movement by Suzanne Cope (non-fiction)Journey back with us to the summer of 1969 with the story of the free breakfast movement, grocery giveaways and other nurturing mutual aid programs started by the Black Panthers. “NYU writing professor Cope rescues two female activists from obscurity in this intriguing look at the role that food played in the civil rights and Black Power movements.” If you read One Crazy Summer with us in 2025, you’ll enjoy learning the backstory to the Panthers’ food program. September 1, 2026: Kasher in the Rye by Moshe Kasher (memoir)Go back to school with this “hilarious memoir about the absurdity of addiction.” “When he was a young boy, Kasher’s mother took him on a vacation to the West Coast. Well it was more like an abduction…They moved to Oakland, California. That’s where the real fun begins, in the war zone of Oakland Public Schools. Those early years read like part Augusten Burroughs, part David Sedaris, with a touch of Jim Carrol….Brutally honest and laugh-out-loud funny, Kasher…finds humor in even the most horrifying situations.” October 6, 2026: 107 Days by Kamala Harris (memoir)Hopefully by October the miles-long hold list for this book will have settled down enough for you to get a copy! “For the first time, and with surprising and revealing insights, Kamala Harris tells the story of one of the wildest and most consequential presidential campaigns in American history.” (NPR) November 3, 2026: The Giveaway: The Clay Blackburn Story by Owen Hill (crime fiction)We have something noir-ish by an Oakland author for those long November nights. “Hill’s crime fiction is whip-smart, stylish, subversive, hilarious and just dripping with East Bay color, culture and landmarks. Sweet and sour like a perfect Negroni, Hill has invented a new kind of East Bay Noir — the fog rolls in late, the golden afternoon glow somehow both luxurious and ominous in the class war that never sleeps.” (Recommendation from Tally Ho! Books via Oaklandside) December 1, 2026: Sign My Name to Freedom by Betty Reid Soskin (memoir)Reid Soskin, famous for being the oldest park ranger in the history of the National Park Service while working at the Rosie the Riveter National Park in Richmond, has been witness to a wide sweep of American history. She grew up in the Bay Area, raised four kids, ran a record store in Berkeley and more. “Betty just turned 104 and her memoir gives a fascinating look at African Americans migration, the Rosie the Riveter story, and beyond through the Civil Rights era.” (recommender) (Ebook and audiobook listed separately) « See our 2025 books here »« See our 2024 books here »« See our 2023 books here »Learn more about Montclair Library and its Friends group, FOML, here: About | Friends of Montclair Library

events.title: Summer 2026 Plant a Seed, Read: Sci-Fi & Fantasy Book Club
events.description: For our Summer Reading event: Plant a Seed, Read we will be discussing Uprooted by Naomi Novik. You can register and pick up a copy at the adult reference desk. "Agnieszka loves her valley home, her quiet village, the forests and the bright shining river. But the corrupted Wood stands on the border, full of malevolent power, and its shadow lies over her life. Her people rely on the cold, driven wizard known only as the Dragon to keep its powers at bay. But he demands a terrible price for his help: one young woman handed over to serve him for ten years, a fate almost as terrible as falling to the Wood. The next choosing is fast approaching, and Agnieszka is afraid. She knows--everyone knows--that the Dragon will take Kasia: beautiful, graceful, brave Kasia, all the things Agnieszka isn't, and her dearest friend in the world. And there is no way to save her. But Agnieszka fears the wrong things. For when the Dragon comes, it is not Kasia he will choose"For accessibility accommodations (ex. events.title: The Plot Thickens Mystery Book Discussion: The Decagon House Murders
events.description: Book Cover the Decagon House MurdersThis month the Plot Thickens Mystery Book Discussion will talk about the Japanese cult Murder mystery The Decagon House Murders by by Yukito Ayatsuji, Translated by Ho-Ling Wong. The K-University Mystery Club’s annual trip takes them to the possibly haunted Island of Tsunojima, the site of a series of notorious, unsolved murders. Members of the Mystery Club don’t expect to have to use their amateur sleuthing skills in any real sense until the first member of their party is found dead, and they all realize their lives depend on solving the mystery. But as time goes on, and more members are found murdered, fear, paranoia and suspicion work against the remaining members’ ability to work together to uncover the killer, their motivation and make it home from Tsunojima alive.Meetings of the Plot Thickens Mystery Book Discussion Group are on the first wednesday morning of alternating months at 11am in Room 201B. The Group is sponsored by The Friends of the Cupertino Library.For accessibility accommodations (ex. ASL), please fill out the online form at sccld.org/accessibility 7+days prior to the event. events.title: Next Chapter Book Club Standing in the Light﻿, ﻿The Diary of Catharine Carey Logan: Delaware Valley, Pennsylvania, 1763 by Mary Pope Osborne.
events.description: You're invited to join the Saint Paul Public Library's Next Chapter Book Club as we read Standing in the Light, The Diary of Catharine Carey Logan: Delaware Valley, Pennsylvania, 1763 by Mary Pope Osborne. Since the fall of 2016, Saint Paul Public Library has been a Next Chapter Book Club affiliate. Next Chapter Book Club offers community-based book clubs for adolescents and adults with intellectual and developmental disabilities. Our members (including some who cannot read) love the way books transport them to different worlds where they meet interesting characters and learn about exciting new things. Taking that journey with a group of friends makes it even more delightful and fun. Join us online (click this link to connect to the virtual book club) Wednesdays 5-6 pm until we reach the end of the book. Estimated completion date end of August 2026. Contact Erin at erin.zolotukhin-ridgway@ci.stpaul.mn.us with questions. This book will be shared online during book club.

events.title: Boos & Brews at Bellbrook Brewing Company:  The Reformatory
events.description: Do you want to explore what the horror genre has to offer? Boos and Brews is for you! Join us at the Bellbrook Brewing Company! for Adults 18+.-- This month we are reading The Reformatory, by Tananarive Due.Gracetown, Florida. June 1950. Twelve-year-old Robbie Stephens, Jr., is sentenced to six months at the Gracetown School for Boys, a reformatory, for kicking the son of the largest landowner in town in defense of his older sister, Gloria. So begins Robbie's journey further into the terrors of the Jim Crow South and the very real horror of the school they call The Reformatory. Robbie has a talent for seeing ghosts, or haints. Boys forced to work to remediate their so-called crimes have gone missing, but the haints Robbie sees hint at worse things. Through his friends Redbone and Blue, Robbie is learning not just the rules but how to survive. Meanwhile, Gloria is rallying every family member and connection in Florida to find a way to get Robbie out before it's too late. Pick up your copy at the Winters Community Library Reference Desk. Boos and Brews meets in the loft at Bellbrook Brewing Company, located at 26 East Franklin Street, Bellbrook, Ohio 45305. A variety of beverages are available for purchase from Bellbrook Brewing Company, To receive updates regarding this event, please register.

events.title: Silent Book Club
events.description: Do you find it difficult to find time to read? Do you hate reading assigned books? Are you anxious in a traditional book club setting? If you answered yes to any of these, then check out Silent Book Club. A new type of book club, where there's no assigned reading and all readers are welcome.BYOBook (print, eBooks, audiobooks, textbooks, comic books, etc.) and settle in for an hour of silent, sustained reading. At the end of the hour, attendees can discuss what they're reading—or not! A fun way to hear about what others are reading and share what you're reading. Best of all, there's dedicated time each month to read something that interests you. Silent Book Club meets the first Wednesday of the month in Creativity Commons on Level 1.

events.title: Bookin' and Cookin': Writers and Lovers by Lily King
events.description: Join us for a discussion of Writers & Lovers by Lily King. Bring food related to the book to share! --- Blindsided by her mother's sudden death, and wrecked by a recent love affair, Casey Peabody has arrived in Massachusetts in the summer of 1997 without a plan. Her mail consists of wedding invitations and final notices from debt collectors. A former child golf prodigy, she now waits tables in Harvard Square and rents a tiny, moldy room at the side of a garage where she works on the novel she's been writing for six years. At thirty-one, Casey is still clutching onto something nearly all her old friends have let go of: the determination to live a creative life. When she falls for two very different men at the same time, her world fractures even more. Casey's fight to fulfill her creative ambitions and balance the conflicting demands of art and life is challenged in ways that push her to the brink. Writers & Lovers follows Casey - a smart and achingly vulnerable protagonist - in the last days of a long youth, a time when every element of her life comes to a crisis. Written with King's trademark humor, heart, and intelligence, Writers & Lovers is a transfixing novel that explores the terrifying and exhilarating leap between the end of one phase of life and the beginning of another.

events.title: Book Banter: The Berlin Letters
events.description: Join us for a fun, engaging discussion of The Berlin Letters, by Katherine Reay. --- Near the end of the Cold War, a CIA code breaker discovers a symbol she recognizes from her childhood, which launches her across the world to the heart of Berlin just before the wall comes tumbling down. November 1989 —After finding a secret cache of letters with intelligence buried in the text, CIA cryptographer Luisa Voekler learns that not only is her father alive but he is languishing in an East German Stasi jail. After piecing together the letters with a series of articles her grandfather saved, Luisa seeks out journalists Bran Bishop and Daniel Rudd. They send her to the CIA, to Andrew Cademan—her boss. Luisa confronts Cademan and learns that nothing is a coincidence, but he will not help her free her father. So she takes matters into her own hands, empties her bank account, and flies to West Berlin. As the adrenaline wears off and she recognizes she has no idea how to proceed, Luisa is both relieved and surprised when a friend shows up with contacts and a rudimentary plan to sneak her across the wall. Alternating storylines between Luisa and her father, The Berlin Letters shows the tumultuous early days of the wall, bringing Berlin, the epicenter of the Cold War, to life while also sharing one family’s journey through secrets, lies, and division to love, freedom, and reconciliation.

Log in to Roblox using your personal account, or if you’re at the library, one of our special CPL accounts.Search for CPLBLOX, our official Roblox experience.CPLBLOX will drop your player avatar into a replica of the Children's Library. events.title: Between the Sheets: A Romance Book Club
events.description: If you love talking tropes, subgenres, HEA versus HFN then this is the book club for you. Every month we'll read a different romance book and come together to discuss what we loved or hated. Books will range from closed door to explicit and explore a wide variety of subgenres.This month's title is Bride by Ali Hazelwood. Misery Lark, the only daughter of the most powerful Vampyre councilman of the Southwest, is an outcast-again. Her days of living in anonymity among the Humans are over: she has been called upon to uphold a historic peacekeeping alliance between the Vampyres and their mortal enemies, the Weres, and she sees little choice but to surrender herself in the exchange. Again. Weres are ruthless and unpredictable, and their Alpha, Lowe Moreland, is no exception. He rules his pack with absolute authority, but not without justice. And, unlike the Vampyre Council, not without feeling. It's clear from the way he tracks Misery's every movement that he doesn't trust her. If only he knew how right he was.... Because Misery has her own reasons to agree to this marriage of convenience, reasons that have nothing to do with politics or alliances, and everything to do with the only thing she's ever cared about. And she is willing to do whatever it takes to get back what's hers, even if it means a life alone in Were territory...alone with the wolf. Steam Level: 🔥🔥🔥🔥4/5 DML welcomes everyone to our programs. If you have questions or need any accommodations, please contact us so we can help make the program a good experience for you. Check out our social story to familiarize yourself with the Miami Township Branch!

events.title: Chinese Culture Club: Who Stole Taiwan's Gold?
events.description: 誰偷走了台灣的黃金主講人:朱力揚 本次展卷社邀請到朱力揚先生分享彼著「誰偷走了台灣的黃金」。此書以美國國家檔案館（NARA）所藏約三千餘頁相關史料為基礎，透過法庭紀錄與官方文件，重構1945 年戰後初期台灣在中、日、美三方交接背景下，一起涉及美軍聯絡人員貪瀆的事件。書中著重於史料的整理與歷史情境的還原，呈現當時政治、軍事與制度交錯下的複雜氛圍，並提供一個有別於既有敘事的理解角度，協助讀者重新思考戰後台灣的脈絡。本演講將以公共史學的方式，介紹相關史料的背景、內容與歷史意義，期能補充當前文化場域中對戰後台灣歷史的理解，提供大眾一個理性、可討論的歷史視角。作者朱力揚，台北空小 33 屆 1966，新竹交大 1976，其他著作 : 「中國空軍抗戰記憶」2015 。 Who Stole Taiwan's Gold?Speaker: Li Yang ChuMr. Chu published his book “Who Stole Taiwan’s Gold?” in 2025 based on thousands of pages of historical materials held at the U.S. National Archives (NARA). He reconstructed an incident involving corruption by U.S. military liaison personnel during post-World War II the trilateral transition of China, Japan and the US in 1945. This book emphasizes the restoration of historical context; presents the complex atmosphere of the intertwined political, military and institutional factors of the time; offers a different perspective from existing narratives and helps readers to rethink the trajectory of postwar Taiwan. In this event, author Mr. Li Yang Chu will share the background, content, and significance of relevant materials in a historical manner to help audience understand postwar Taiwanese history. Author Li Yang Chu, an alumnus of Taipei Air Force Elementary School (Class of 1966) and National Chiao Tung University (Class of 1976). He also published the book "Memories of the Chinese Air Force in the War of Resistance Against Japan" (2015). This program will be in Mandarin.

events.title: Food For Thought: A Culinary Book Club
events.description: Indulge in your passion for literature and cuisine at a unique book club where we combine the love of reading with the love of food! For August, we're going to read different books based on a specific theme chosen for the month. Come ready to talk about the book you have read and get some new ideas for your own reading. We are still going to be making and eating a delicious snack loosely inspired by the monthly theme. Don't miss out on this opportunity to bond with fellow book-lovers over a shared love of literature and food.The August theme will be dystopian/utopian novels. Come ready to talk about a book of your choice! We have provided some suggestions below. All titles are available to borrow from the library. To accompany our book talks we will be making and drinking Soylent green smoothies. How High We go in the Dark; Sequoia Nagamatsu, 2022 To Paradise; Hanya Yanagihara, 2022 The Seep; Chana Porter, 2020 Station Eleven; Emily St. John Mandel, 2014 The Madd Addam Trilogy: Oryx and Crake; The Year of the Flood; MaddAddam; Margaret Atwood, 2003-2009 Parable of the Sower; Octavia Butler, 2000 The Handmaid’s Tale; Margaret Atwood, 1985 Dune; Frank Herbert, 1965 Herland; Charlotte Perkins Gilman, 1915 Utopia, Thomas More, 1516 NOTE: Registration is required and there is a $5 charge for this class. Payment can be made via Zeffy. A link will be sent to the email address you provide when you complete the registration process on this page.

events.title: Nonfiction Book Discussion: Rough Sleepers by Tracy Kidder
events.description: Open to all adults who love to read and discuss nonfictional literature. in August, the nonfiction book discussion group with talk about Rough Sleepers: Dr. Jim O'Connell's Urgent Mission to Bring Healing to Homeless People by Tracy Kidder. (2023)When Dr. Jim O’Connell, a Harvard Medical School graduate was close to the end of his residency, he accepted a proposal from the head of Massachusetts General Hospital to serve for a year as a physician to Boston’s homeless population also known as the Rough Sleepers. What started as a challenge he didn't feel he could refuse became Dr. O’Connell’s calling, and forty years later, he and multiple teams of physicians from Massachusetts General Hospital and the Boston Medical Center are still serving the City’s homeless population from a fleet of medically equipped vans, seeking their patients in the streets at night, bringing not only needed medical care, but hope to the people in greatest need. The nonfiction book discussion group meets on the second Sunday morning of each month at 11am in Room 201B. Sponsored by The Friends of the Cupertino LibraryFor accessibility accommodations (ex. ASL), please fill out the online form at sccld.org/accessibility 7+days prior to the event. If you need assistance with the form, speak with a staff member at your local library, call (408) 540-3945, or email us at ask@sccl.org.

events.title: Leemos en Español
events.description: Cada año, la Biblioteca Pública de Omaha invita a la comunidad a leer el mismo libro como parte de Omaha Lee. Este año te invitamos a leer Criaturas luminosas de Shelby Van Pelt. Acompáñanos este mes para una conversación sobre el libro seleccionado para Omaha Lee. Las reuniones de los clubes de lectura de OPL son dirigidas por el personal de la biblioteca y están abiertas para todas las personas. Esta conmovedora novela cuenta la historia de una viuda que forma una amistad inesperada con un pulpo gigante del Pacífico que vive en un acuario local. A través de esta relación, descubre verdades sobre la desaparición de su hijo ocurrida hace 30 años. Omaha Lee conecta a la comunidad por medio de la lectura y crea oportunidades para conversar y compartir ideas. Cada año, el libro se elige mediante una votación pública y se promueve a través de charlas sobre libros, visitas de autores y programas relacionados. Puedes reservar una copia de Criaturas luminosas en el catálogo de la Biblioteca Pública de Omaha. Todas las sucursales de la Biblioteca Pública de Omaha son accesibles para personas en silla de ruedas. ¿Necesita interpretación en lenguaje de señas u otro tipo de asistencia para accesibilidad en este evento? Por favor, envíe un correo electrónico a Nancy Novotny a NNovotny@omahalibrary.org para solicitar adaptaciones. Las solicitudes deben hacerse al menos cinco días hábiles antes de la actividad o evento.

events.title: Movie: Holes
events.description: Rated G | 2003 | 1h 57min Escape the heat (or gloom) with a family movie matinee!One day, after Stanley is falsely accused of stealing a pair of sneakers, he is sentenced to 18 months at Camp Green Lake. But when he gets there, he discovers that this camp is really more like a prison.Based on the award-winning book by Louis Sachar, who also wrote the screenplay, Holes is a fun and poignant adventure that tells the story of Stanley Yelnats (Shia LaBeouf), an unusual young hero. Dogged by bad luck stemming from an ancient family curse, Stanley has little clue that he's in for the adventure of his life when sent to Camp Green Lake, where he and his campmates are mysteriously forced by the camp's director (Sigourney Weaver) and her right-hand men Mr. Sir (Jon Voight) and Mr. Pendanski (Tim Blake Nelson) to dig holes in order "to build character." None of the boys know the real reason they're digging all these holes, but Stanley soon begins to question why the head of the camp is so interested in anything "special" the boys find. Through it all, Stanley and his campmates must stick together as they attempt to solve the mystery and break the Yelnats family curse. Tickets given out 30 minutes before showtime.

events.title: Lynnwood Library Book Group - "How to Age Disgracefully" by Clare Pooley
events.description: Join us to celebrate the love of reading with others in our community! The Lynnwood Library Book Group meets regularly on the second Monday of each month from 6:30-7:45pm. This month's selection is How to Age Disgracefully by Clare Pooley. Books are selected by members of the group and cover a variety of genres. When Lydia takes a job running the Senior Citizens' Social Club three afternoons a week, she assumes she'll be spending her time drinking tea and playing gentle games of cards. The members of the Social Club, however, are not at all what Lydia was expecting. From Art, a failed actor turned kleptomaniac to Daphne, who has been hiding from her dark past for decades to Ruby, a Banksy-style knitter who gets revenge in yarn, these seniors look deceptively benign--but when age makes you invisible, secrets are so much easier to hide. When the city council threatens to sell the doomed community center building, the members of the Social Club join forces with their tiny friends in the daycare next door--as well as the teenaged father of one of the toddlers and a geriatric dog--to save the building. Together, this group's unorthodox methods may actually work, as long as the police don't catch up with them first.View all of Lynnwood Library Book Group's 2026 selections.Contact us with questions Programs & Events FAQs To request a sign language interpreter, complete this online form or contact your local library at least two weeks prior to the library event. Interpreters will be provided based on availability.

events.title: Book Buzz: To the Moon and Back
events.description: Join us to discuss To the Moon and Back by Eliana Ramage. --Copies of the book will be reserved and available at the library and electronic versions can be found on Hoopla or Libby.Registration for this event is encouraged but not required.Steph Harper is convinced that only space—outer space—can save her. From a childhood of fearful running and alienation; from a family and community that threaten to suffocate her with their reverence for the past. Equal parts tender, funny, and heartbreaking, To the Moon and Back charts the course of Steph’s singular dream: to become the first Cherokee astronaut, no matter who or what she has to leave behind.But despite her self-prescribed loneliness and reckless ambition, Steph’s story isn’t hers alone. To the Moon and Back also brings to life the vibrant, complex women—a celebrity activist younger sister, an ex-Mormon college girlfriend, and a devoted mother with a crushing secret—who insist on loving her…even when she least deserves them.From a simulated Mars habitat on a Hawaiian volcano, to a house in the Ozark foothills in Cherokee Nation, to a pressurized research station on the floor of the Atlantic and beyond, Steph will stretch her bonds with each of these women to the point of breaking, driving them to reconsider their own deepest desires in her shadow. An awe-inspiringly epic novel of mothers and daughters, sisters and sacrifice, love and loss, terror and wonder, To the Moon and Back is the unforgettable story of one astronaut’s most surprising discovery: how deeply she loves life on earth.

events.title: Benson Mystery Book Club
events.description: Each year, Omaha Public Library encourages the community to join together in reading one book as part of Omaha Reads. This year we invite you to read "Remarkably Bright Creatures” by Shelby Van Pelt. Join us this month for a lively discussion of the Omaha Reads title. OPL Book Club discussions are led by OPL staff and are open to everyone. A luminous debut novel about a widow's unlikely friendship with a giant Pacific octopus reluctantly residing at the local aquarium-and the truths she finally uncovers about her son's disappearance 30 years ago. Omaha Reads promotes literacy and provides the City with a common theme to discuss. One book is selected through a public vote and promoted through book talks, author visits, and related programs. Copies of “Remarkably Bright Creatures” can be reserved for checkout from the OPL catalog. All Omaha Public Library locations are wheelchair accessible. Need sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please email Nancy Novotny at NNovotny@omahalibrary.org to request accommodation. events.title: Book Clubs of EPL: Bring Your Own!  Death and Dying
events.description: Book Clubs of EPL - Bring Your Own! is a participant-led gathering where Edmontonians share and discuss the books they’re currently reading. Join us in to exchange ideas, discover new titles, and connect with fellow readers in a friendly and engaging environment. Clareview Branch is taking a short deviation from our usual book club format, where we all read the same book title (don't worry, we will again!), and will be discussing what each participant chooses to bring to the meeting and share with the group. August's theme is: Death and Dying book share. You are invited to bring your favrourite or recent read which deals with issues surrounding death and dying. Bring anything from fiction to non-fiction, poetry to fairy tales, or even a graphic novel. We would love your perspective on this important, relevant, and often neglected topic of discussion.Join us!

events.title: Sounds from the Valley: Music and Poetry
events.description: Head down to Edmonton's Snow Valley Ski Club to take in a FREE concert with music, poetry and art! Listen to artists from EPL's Capital City Records and Capital City Press as they share their work at the bottom of the ski hill in the beautiful Rainbow Valley. Free of charge, we'll be collecting non-perishable food items for the Edmonton Food Bank.NOTE: Due to the very high popularity of the event this year, we suggest those who are able to bring their own lawn chairs to do so to ensure ample seating for everyone. If you don’t have a lawn chair, that is totally okay – we can absolutely make it work!Music from Capital City Records Artist, Jon MartinJon Martin is a songwriter, producer, and musician from southern Alberta. He studied music and music psychology at the degree and master's level, spent years running a recording studio in Lethbridge producing records for other artists, and relocated to Edmonton in early 2021. Producer Gethin Pearson (Charli XCX, Badly Drawn Boy) described his music as "timeless capsules... whilst also feeling fresh and from your life."His 2022 album This Could Be Our Year — self-produced and built around an international cast of collaborators — was named one of the Edmonton Journal's 10 Best Local Releases of the year and was jury-selected for the Edmonton Public Library's Capital City Records collection in 2024. Over seven full-length albums and several EPs, his records have earned airplay across Canada, including on CBC Radio One."Part-Time Hero," a collaborative R&B-leaning duet released in March 2026, marks a different direction. Also in March 2026, a live concert in Edmonton was recorded for broadcast on CBC Radio One's Provincial Playlist Live. New singles are in progress, a follow-up to Late September Garden Party is in the works, and a separate album of music about space is also on the way.”Readings from Capital City Press Authors:Theodora “Theo” Sterling (she/her) is a queer writer from Alberta who always carries a fountain pen and journal everywhere she goes. Her work has appeared in WestWord Magazine, Funicular, FEELS Zine, and elsewhere. Occasionally, she posts her words (and cat photos) on Instagram @CupsOfSilver. patti sinclair’s poetry publications include the bestselling book: The Late Season (At Bay Press, 2025) a narrative long poem, diving into how death re-stories our lives. More? poet-at-large.blogspirit.com & @locating the beauty

events.title: The Page Turners
events.description: The Page Turners is an evening book discussion group for adults that meets at the Castro Valley Library on the second Tuesday of the month at 7 pm. Facilitated by librarian Chris Selig, the discussions focus on compelling, well-reviewed fiction and nonfiction books written for adults.For August we will discuss Our Town: A Play in Three Acts by Thornton Wilder.SynopsisOur Town explores the relationship between two young neighbors, George Gibbs and Emily Webb, whose childhood friendship blossoms into romance, and then culminates in marriage. When Emily loses her life during childbirth, the circle of life portrayed in each of the three acts--childhood, adulthood, and death --is fully realized. Our Town is a play. We have the print version available in the Library and through Link Plus. You can read any edition. You can also watch a recording of the play staged in 1976 at Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts. It can be found in the Alexander Street Performing Arts Collection, in the Library's eLibrary. A library card and PIN is required.Our Town _____________________________Library staff may take photos, videos, or audio recordings at our events. Please tell us if you do not wish to be photographed, video-recorded, or audio-recorded. We will always respect your requests. An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter can be provided for this program if requested at least seven business days in advance. For assistance, please contact your library or accessibility@aclibrary.org.

events.title: Next Chapter Book Club Standing in the Light﻿, ﻿The Diary of Catharine Carey Logan: Delaware Valley, Pennsylvania, 1763 by Mary Pope Osborne.
events.description: You're invited to join the Saint Paul Public Library's Next Chapter Book Club as we read Standing in the Light, The Diary of Catharine Carey Logan: Delaware Valley, Pennsylvania, 1763 by Mary Pope Osborne. Since the fall of 2016, Saint Paul Public Library has been a Next Chapter Book Club affiliate. Next Chapter Book Club offers community-based book clubs for adolescents and adults with intellectual and developmental disabilities. Our members (including some who cannot read) love the way books transport them to different worlds where they meet interesting characters and learn about exciting new things. Taking that journey with a group of friends makes it even more delightful and fun. Join us online (click this link to connect to the virtual book club) Wednesdays 5-6 pm until we reach the end of the book. Estimated completion date end of August 2026. Contact Erin at erin.zolotukhin-ridgway@ci.stpaul.mn.us with questions. This book will be shared online during book club.

events.title: Fantasy and Science Fiction Book Club: The Last Murder at the End of the World by Stuart Turton
events.description: Join us on the second Wednesday each month to discuss a fantasy or science fiction book! --- This month's title is The Last Murder at the End of the World by Stuart Turton.From the bestselling author of The 7 1/2 Deaths of Evelyn Hardcastle and The Devil and the Dark Water comes an inventive, high-concept murder mystery: an ingenious puzzle, an extraordinary backdrop, and an audacious solution. Solve the murder to save what's left of the world. Outside the island there is nothing: the world was destroyed by a fog that swept the planet, killing anyone it touched. On the island: it is idyllic. One hundred and twenty-two villagers and three scientists, living in peaceful harmony. The villagers are content to fish, farm and feast, to obey their nightly curfew, to do what they're told by the scientists. Until, to the horror of the islanders, one of their beloved scientists is found brutally stabbed to death. And then they learn that the murder has triggered a lowering of the security system around the island, the only thing that was keeping the fog at bay. If the murder isn't solved within 92 hours, the fog will smother the island-and everyone on it. But the security system has also wiped everyone's memories of exactly what happened the night before, which means that someone on the island is a murderer-and they don't even know it. And the clock is ticking

events.description: The Unplugged Book Club is a collaborative book discussion series between ODC Network and Herrick District Library. Throughout 2026, we invite you to read books related to disconnecting from unhealthy tech habits and reconnecting with our community and nature. Want to meet some of the authors? Join the ODC Network's Unplugged: Disconnect to Reconnect speaker series to meet Catherine Price (January 14), Katherine Martinko (February 12), Dr. Scott Sampson (March 3), and Lenore Skenazy (March 17).About the Unplugged: Disconnect to Reconnect speaker series - ODC Network invites the West Michigan community to disconnect from overuse of technology and reconnect with nature and each other, while reintroducing the simple idea that outdoor and community connection is central to thriving people, thriving families, and thriving communities. Learn more at outdoordiscovery.org/unplugged Here is our Unplugged Book Club lineup: August 13th, 10:00-11:00a Herrick Main (classroom) – Digital Minimalism by Cal Newport ___ September 17th, 6:30- 7:30p at Herrick North Branch – The Nature Fix by Florence Williams ___ October 7th, 6:00-7:00p at Herrick Main (classroom) – My Side of the Mountain by Jean Craighead George (All ages, with Youth Services) ___ November 10th, 6:00-7:00p at Herrick Main (classroom) – Wild by Cheryl Strayed

events.title: Getting Cozy Book Club
events.description: If you've never read a cozy mystery before or if you have read thousands, join us each month as we read and discuss the first book in a series. We will meet on the 2nd Thursday of the month at 10 am. What is a cozy mystery? The crime-solver is an amateur sleuth, who eavesdrops, gathers clues, and uses their native intelligence and knowledge of their community to solve the crime. Cozy mysteries are generally considered “gentle” books and most have no graphic violence. The setting is often a small town full of colorful characters.Series frequently have a prominent thematic element introduced by the detective's job, pet, or hobby.These books are plot and character driven with an emphasis on solving puzzles rather than suspense. Our chosen book with serve as our theme for the meeting, we will talk about other book series with a similar theme as well as the recipes, crafts, and more included in many cozies.

events.title: Civic Center Morning Mystery Book Group: 'The Mistress of Bhatia House' by Sujata Massey
events.description: The Civic Center Library's Morning Mystery Book Group will meet on Thursday, August 13th from 11:00am-12:00pm to discuss The Mistress of Bhatia House by Sujata Massey. This mystery book group generally meets on the 2nd Thursday of every month from 11:00-12:00n. All are welcome to join! Request a copy of the book online or ask at the Info Desk of the Civic Center Library (or call 415-473-6057) for assistance in placing a hold. The Mistress of Bhatia House (book) or The Mistress of Bhatia House (eBook) or The Mistress of Bhatia House (eAudiobook) by Sujata MasseyBombay’s only female solicitor, Perveen Mistry, confronts class divisions, sexism, and complex family dynamics as she seeks justice for a mistreated young woman. India, 1922: Perveen Mistry is the only female lawyer in Bombay, a city where child mortality is high, birth control is unavailable and very few women have ever seen a doctor.Perveen is attending a lavish fundraiser for a new women’s hospital specializing in maternal health issues when she witnesses an accident. The grandson of an influential Gujarati businessman catches fire—but his young servant, Sunanda, rushes to save him, selflessly putting herself in harm’s way. Later, Perveen learns that Sunanda, who’s recovering from her burns, has been arrested on fabricated charges made by a man who doesn’t seem to exist. Perveen cannot stand by while Sunanda languishes in jail with no hope of justice. She takes Sunanda as a client, inviting her to live at the Mistry home in Bombay’s Dadar Parsi colony. When the hospital’s chief donor dies suddenly, Miriam Penkar, a Jewish-Indian obstetrician, and Sunanda become suspects. Perveen’s original case spirals into a complex investigation taking her into the Gujarati strongholds of Kalbadevi and Ghatkopar, and up the coast to Juhu Beach. Then a second fire erupts, and Perveen realizes how much is at stake. Has someone powerful framed Sunanda to cover up another crime? And will Perveen be able to prove Sunanda’s innocence without endangering her own family? 4th book in the Perveen Mistry Investigations series. - adapted from the publisher ADA Accommodation Request | Solicitud de Adaptación ADA

events.title: Book Clubs of EPL: Being Mortal
events.description: Love reading? Love to talk about books you've read? Come join us for lively discussions on all types of fiction and non-fiction. We'll feature different titles and authors each month. Come and share your impressions, thoughts and interpretations of a book we have all read. This month we will be reading Being Mortal by Atul Gawande. "Being Mortal looks at the way modern medicine has changed the experience of dying, what the implications of this change are for each of us, and what we would need to do to change a system that knows a lot about prolonging life but little about tending to death.At the heart of this book is something larger and more lasting than even its agenda for how to effect change: it is a deeply humane portrayal of how our society copes with who we really are. We are not economic beings. We are not political beings. We are not digital beings or analog beings, social beings or solitary beings. We are mortal beings. And in that is every important thing to know about how we must live."

events.title: August Moraga Book Club
events.description: Join other book lovers for a lively discussion regarding Ray Bradbury's Fahrenheit 451: Set in a dystopian future, Ray Bradbury's classic novel follows fireman Guy Montag, whose job is to burn books and their hiding places. Living a bland life with his wife, Mildred, Montag's perspective shifts after meeting his curious neighbor, Clarisse. She introduces him to a past without fear and the value of ideas found in books, prompting Montag to question everything he knows.Note that Moraga Book Club is for adults ages 18 and up.

Participants must enroll separately for each meeting.Our short stories for July through September 2026 are: July 9, 2026: Civil Peace by Chinua AchebeAugust 13, 2026: Lydia by Zsuzsa Bánk September 10, 2026: A Sound of Thunder by Ray Bradbury Suggestions for short stories for future meetings are encouraged! At the end of each year, we suggest and vote on books to read and sometimes invite local authors to discuss their books. You don't have to register, but you will receive a reminder email if you register. Here's our reading list for 2026: My Friends by Fredrik Bachman - JanuaryThe Wager: A Tale of a Shipwreck, Mutiny and Murder by David Grann - FebruarySouth of the Border, West of the Sun by Haruki Murakami - MarchTrespassers at the Golden Gate: A True Account of Love, Murder, and Madness in Gilded-Age San Francisco by Gary Krist - AprilThe Best We Could Do by Thi Bui - MayThe Wedding People by Alison Espach - JuneUnlikely Animals: A Novel by Annie Hartnett - JulySay Nothing: A True Story of Murder and Memory in Northern Ireland by Patrick Radden Keefe - AugustThe Frozen River by Ariel Lawhon - SeptemberWe'll Prescribe You a Cat by Syou Ishida - OctoberWhat We Can Know by Ian McEwan - NovemberThe Amazing Adventures of Kavalier & Clay by Michael Chabon - December Sign up for the library newsletter and never miss another program!

events.title: Little Makers Club (Ages 0-5)
events.description: This themed, literacy-based drop in program is chock full of activities to help prepare your child for school and beyond. We'll work on motor skills, literacy and counting all while playing in an enriching environment that allows for socialization and for each child to choose their own activity (matching their energy level). Crafts and toddler-friendly snacks will be provided, along with a weekly take-home goody bag to continue learning at home. Manage your event registration through "My Events" when signed in to a library account. DISCOVER MORE Running of out books to read? Ask a librarian for even more personalized recommendations!Visit the Kids Page to discover additional recommendations and resources.

events.title: Folsom Book Club
events.description: Join us on the second Friday of every month from 1-2 PM for book discussion on various genres such as historical fiction, nonfiction, mystery, and suspense.When you attend the meeting, you can check out a copy of the next month's book.Digital copies may also be available through one of St. Tammany Parish Library's eBook/eAudiobook services,Cloud Library , Hoopla, or Libby.

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - Remarkably Bright Creatures
events.description: Rianne Hastie Adams will lead an intermediate ESL Book Club for Remarkably Bright Creatures by Shelby Van Pelt. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Meeting dates: July 24, 31; August 14, 21, 28; September 4, 11, 18 (No class August 7.) Meeting time: Fridays 11:30am - 12:30pm Where: Literacy Table Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. Remarkably Bright Creatures description:After losing her husband, Tova Sullivan works nights at the Sowell Bay Aquarium to cope with the lingering grief of her son Erik’s mysterious disappearance more than thirty years earlier. There, she forms an unlikely bond with Marcellus, a perceptive giant Pacific octopus who uncovers clues about what happened to Erik and becomes determined to reveal the truth. In this warm and uplifting debut novel, Shelby Van Pelt explores friendship, loss, and second chances, showing how confronting the past can open the door to an unexpected future.

events.description: Early readers unite! If your age falls between 5 – 8 bring your curious mind for laughter, learning and lots of fun at this book club. Each month, you’ll dive into a new story. After the story, we’ll chat about what happened and what might come next. But the fun doesn’t stop there! Get hands on and complete an exciting activity inspired by the book!

events.title: Teen Advisory Board @ Main TeenZone
events.description: Interested in creating your own teen programs or events? Got a club you've always wanted to start or a skill you'd love to share? Have an eye for design and ideas for book displays or holiday celebrations? Drop in to our monthly Teen Advisory Board (TAB) meetings on the second *Friday* of the month at the Main Library TeenZone to learn more about what's possible at TAB and start collaborating with other teens. Snacks provided!TAB is a youth-led, librarian-supported volunteer group for youth in 7th—12th grades who are excited to take an active role in shaping what we offer to teens at the Main TeenZone. As a TAB member, you get the opportunity to: Make real your ideas for the TeenZone as a place for teens, by teens! Develop real-world skills and experience that you can use in job and college applications Fulfill your community service hour requirements Enjoy food, fun, and friendship at our monthly meet-ups Fill out a volunteer application here. Get accessibility information.

events.title: Book Bash
events.description: Join fellow book lovers for the in-person Book Bash at OPL's Central Library. Attendees will hear about interesting and exciting books from library staff, have a chance to win bookish prizes, and get to mingle with fellow book lovers. Attendees will each take home a list of their favorite titles. Reading enthusiasts and book clubs are encouraged to attend! Printed Book Bash guides can be picked up at your local OPL branch after Aug 1, while supplies last. Doors open at 6:00 p.m., and the program begins at 6:30 p.m. Select books will be available for checkout. Items from local craft and booksellers will be available for purchase. Soft drinks and light snacks will be available. Registration is required to attend this event. Want to watch from home? Use this Crowdcast link to register and watch from home. All Omaha Public Library locations are wheelchair accessible. Need sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please email Nancy Novotny at NNovotny@omahalibrary.org to request accommodation. Requests must be made at least five business days before the activity or event.

Online attendees will hear about new and exciting books from library staff, have a chance to win bookish prizes, and get to chat virtually with fellow book lovers. Attendees will end the night with a list of their favorite titles from the evening. Printed Book Bash guides can be picked up at your local OPL branch beginning Aug. 1, while supplies last. Reading enthusiasts and book clubs are encouraged to attend. Register on Crowdcast to attend this event. Want to attend this event in-person? Register here for the in-person event at Central Library. All Omaha Public Library locations are wheelchair accessible. Need sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please email Nancy Novotny at NNovotny@omahalibrary.org to request accommodation. Requests must be made at least five business days before the activity or event.

events.title: Bite Sized Book Club - The Hollow Needle
events.description: Think you're too busy for a book club? Think again! Schlafly's "Bite-Sized Books" features diverse selections under 250 pages - a perfect fit for busy students, parents and professionals. Whether you're looking to try new genres, sample a different perspective or fit in a quick read during your kiddo's nap time, this club has you covered. Join us for our Saturday one-hour discussions every month. And if our current theme isn't for you, don't fret - check back in January for our next topic!This unit's focus is "Famous Detectives", and August's selection is "The Hollow Needle" by Maurice Leblanc (207 pages). Description - "The famous gentleman-burglar and master of disguises Arsène Lupin is chasing the most valuable object he has ever had the opportunity to steal: the Hollow Needle, which hides unfathomable treasure and a secret that the kings of France have been handing down since the time of Julius Caesar."

At the program she will discuss the book’s topic with participants as well as lead a fun activity based on the topic. (Adults) Limited numbers of the titles will be available for check-out from the library. Patrons are encouraged to source the titles by other means, if necessary. Summer has been a Naturalist since May 2024. She has a degree in Wildlife Conservation from Zane State College. Summer has worked at The Columbus Zoo and Aquarium as a volunteer, interpreter and zookeeper, in the education department at The Wilds and volunteered with Ohio Nature Education, taking care of birds of prey and mammals. She’s very passionate about outdoor education, animal care and bringing the community together!

events.title: Plains Pages Book Club
events.description: Each year, Omaha Public Library encourages the community to join together in reading one book as part of Omaha Reads. This year we invite you to read "Remarkably Bright Creatures” by Shelby Van Pelt. Join us this month for a lively discussion of the Omaha Reads title. OPL Book Club discussions are led by OPL staff and are open to everyone. A luminous debut novel about a widow's unlikely friendship with a giant Pacific octopus reluctantly residing at the local aquarium-and the truths she finally uncovers about her son's disappearance 30 years ago. Omaha Reads promotes literacy and provides the City with a common theme to discuss. One book is selected through a public vote and promoted through book talks, author visits, and related programs. Copies of “Remarkably Bright Creatures” can be reserved for checkout from the OPL catalog. All Omaha Public Library locations are wheelchair accessible. Need sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please email Nancy Novotny at NNovotny@omahalibrary.org to request accommodation. July 11 - In the Country We Love by Diane Guerrero August 15 - Butter by Asako Yuzuki September 12 - The Lion Women of Tehran by Marjan KamaliOctober 10 - Crying in H Mart by Michelle ZaunerNovember 14 - Killers of the Flower Moon by David Grann

events.title: The Chismosas Only Book Club with Laekan Zea Kemp (Hybrid Program)
events.description: Join Nuestras Raíces for an afternoon with award-winning author Laeken Zea Kemp to discuss her latest book, The Chismosas Only Book Club. The author will be joining us virtually for a book club Q & A discussion led by members of the Nuestras Raíces team, which will take place at Richard Elías Mission Library. Community members can participate in a Q&A while enjoying a fun game of Lotería and more! We will also be giving away copies of the book on a first-come, first-served basis. Please note: This is a hybrid event! There are two ways you can register: for the in-person event or to attend the webinar online. Please do not register for both. In-person attendees should register through the library website in the top right of the event.Zoom Webinar attendees should register through our Zoom registration link below: https://us02web.zoom.us/webinar/register... Participants joining us in person can play Lotería and pick up a copy of the book while supplies last. Virtual attendees can look forward to a chisme-filled discussion with the author. Author Bio: Laekan Zea Kemp is a writer living in Austin, Texas. Her debut novel, Somewhere Between Bitter and Sweet, was a 2022 Pura Belpré Honor Recipient. She has three objectives when it comes to storytelling: to make people laugh, cry, and crave Mexican food. Her work celebrates Chicane grit, resilience, creativity, and joy while exploring themes of identity and mental health.

Rainbow Reads is a safe place for all.Our August read: The Great Disillusionment of Nick and Jay Note: this event is held via Zoom. Please register to receive login information the day before our conversation. Get recommended reads from Pride and see what's new in our collection on our Pride page.

events.title: Queer Readers' Cafe
events.description: Queer joy shall abound at the Queer Readers’ Café! The Queer Readers' Cafe is sort of like a silent book club, but chatter is OK. Unlike a typical book club, participants can read whatever they want! We’ll create an LGBTQ+ friendly space in our community room for folks to gather, read, and discuss what they’re reading. A selection of queer reading materials will be available to browse and checkout. Light snacks and refreshments will be provided. Sharing and meeting like-minded readers is encouraged, but participants will not be asked to disclose their identities or their reading preferences. While the intention of this program is to highlight books about the LGBTQ+ experience and/or by LGBTQ+ authors, and to create a safe space for patrons identifying as LGBTQ+, we welcome all participants and reading materials. ADA Accommodation Requests ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Café de Lectores Queer ¡La alegría queer abundará en el Café del libro de lectores queer! El Café de lectores queer es como un club de lectura silencioso, pero se permite charlar. A diferencia de un club de lectura típico, ¡los participantes pueden leer lo que quieran! Crearemos un espacio amigable LGBTQ+ en nuestra sala comunitaria para que la gente pueda reunirse, leer y discutir lo que está leyendo. Habrá una selección de materiales de lectura queer disponibles para consultar y consultar. Se proporcionarán aperitivos ligeros y refrescos. Se recomienda compartir y conocer lectores con ideas afines, pero no se pedirá a los participantes que revelen sus identidades ni sus preferencias de lectura. Aunque este programa tiene como objetivo destacar libros sobre la experiencia LGBTQ+ y/o de autores LGBTQ+, y crear un espacio seguro para los usuarios que se identifican como LGBTQ+, damos la bienvenida a todos los participantes y materiales de lectura.Solicitudes de adaptaciones ADA

events.title: Philosophy For Life
events.description: Philosophy for Life A friendly reading and discussion group at the library, exploring big ideas about how we live and what we value. Why do we believe that success means constant hustle, that more is always better, or that everyone must go it alone? What other ways of thinking might help us make sense of everyday life? Together, we’ll read accessible texts from different philosophical traditions and enjoy open, respectful conversations about the assumptions shaping our world. Open to adults, young adults and seniors. No background in philosophy required—just curiosity and a willingness to talk and listen. We’ll read the following books: Living for Pleasure: An Epicurean Guide to Life by Emily A. Austin A Guide to the Good Life: The Ancient Art of Stoic Joy by William B. Irvine Choosing Freedom: A Kantian Guide to Life by Karen Stohr On Being and Becoming: an Existentialist Approach to Life by Jennifer Anna Gosetti-Ferencei Blooming in the Ruins: How Mexican Philosophy Can Guide Us Toward the Good Life by Carlos Alberto Sánchez We’ll meet every first and third Sunday at the Rinconada Library from 3:30 PM to 4:45 PM.*A detailed list of readings for each meeting can be found on the library calendar under the date of the corresponding meeting. Moderated by Natalia & Lottie, PACL staff. The first meeting is on Sunday, August 16th. Topic: Philosophy as a Way of Life “Vain is the word of a philosopher, by which no moral suffering is healed. Just as medicine confers no benefit if it does not drive away bodily disease, so is philosophy useless if it does not drive away the suffering of the mind.” Epicurus, Fragment “On Philosophy.” “Philosophy’s main task is to respond to the soul’s cry; to make sense of and thereby free ourselves from the hold of our griefs and fears.” Epictetus, The Manual, “The Soul’s Cry.” Literature: Epicurus. The Art of Happiness. Penguin Books, 2012. Letter to Menoeceus, 122;Epictetus. The Art of Living: The Classical manual on Virtue, Happiness, and Effectiveness. HarperSanFrancisco, 1995. “Living Wisdom Is More Important Than Knowing About It,” p. 77, “The Real Purpose of Philosophy, ” pp. 84-85;Seneca. Letters from a Stoic. Penguin Classics, 2025. Letter XVI, pp. 47-50.* *All texts will also be provided at the meeting. Some Questions to Discuss: What is the difference between knowing and being?What can it mean to live a human life?Can a way of (good/happy) life be taught? Should it be taught?Can philosophy teach it?

events.description: Ursula K. Le Guin’s “The Lathe of Heaven” Have an appetite for science fiction but don't have time to delve into another space opera? Join us every second Monday of the month to discuss the novellas and short story collections of award-winning sci-fi authors. Interested participants are welcome to pick up their copies of this month’s book at Divoll. For August, we’re reading The Lathe of Heaven by the iconic sci-fi and fantasy author Ursula K. Le Guin. Set in a near-future Portland, Oregon, Le Guin’s protagonist realizes he can change reality through his dreams. Meanwhile, the psychiatrist from whom he seeks help, begins to manipulate this power for his own nefarious purposes.For accessibility needs, please call 314.241.2288

This week we will be reading This Place:150 Years Retold by Kateri Akiwenzie-Damm"Explore the last 150 years through the eyes of Indigenous creators in this groundbreaking graphic novel anthology. Beautifully illustrated, these stories are an emotional and enlightening journey through magic realism, serial killings, psychic battles, and time travel. events.title: Book Clubs of EPL: Tom Lake
events.description: Love reading? Love to talk about books you've read? Come join us for lively discussions on all types of fiction and non-fiction. We'll feature different titles and authors each month. Join us to share your impressions, thoughts and interpretations of a book we have all read. Tom Lake by Ann Patchett In the spring of 2020, Lara's three daughters return to the family's orchard in Northern Michigan. While picking cherries, they beg their mother to tell them the story of Peter Duke, a famous actor with whom she shared both a stage and a romance years before at a theater company called Tom Lake. As Lara recalls the past, her daughters examine their own lives and relationship with their mother, and are forced to reconsider the world and everything they thought they knew. Tom Lake is a meditation on youthful love, married love, and the lives parents have led before their children were born. Both hopeful and elegiac, it explores what it means to be happy even when the world is falling apart. As in all of her novels, Ann Patchett combines compelling narrative artistry with piercing insights into family dynamics. The result is a rich and luminous story, told with profound intelligence and emotional subtlety, that demonstrates once again why she is one of the most revered and acclaimed literary talents working today.

events.title: Manchester Book Group
events.description: Pick up the next book club selection at the Manchester library service desk following the previous month's meeting on the 3rd Monday of the month. **NOTE Meetings in January and February each year are held on the FOURTH Monday of the month due to the Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Day and President's Day holidays This month we are discussing: Come and Get It by Kiley ReidFrom the Publisher: Acclaimed author Kiley Reid’s fresh and provocative story about desire, consumption, and bad behavior.It’s 2017 at the University of Arkansas, and Millie Cousins—a super-senior resident assistant at Belgrade Dormitory—just wants to graduate, get a job, and buy a house. So when Agatha Paul, a writer and visiting professor itching for her next big topic, offers Millie an easy yet unusual opportunity for them to help each other further their own interests, Millie naturally jumps at the chance.But Millie’s starry-eyed hustle quickly becomes jeopardized by a lonely transfer student, unruly residents, and illicit intrigue. Both Millie and Agatha are forced to question just how much of themselves they are willing to trade to get what they want.Sharp and intimate, Come and Get It, the new thought-provoking, singular novel by the bestselling and critically acclaimed author Kiley Reid, explores the choices we make, particularly for the things that can and cannot be paid for.

events.title: Author Talk with Jen Fisher
events.description: Hope is the Strategy: Why Possibility is a Skill, Not a Feeling Author Jen Fisher is a global authority on workplace wellbeing and founder and CEO of the The Wellbeing Team. She is Deloitte US's first wellbeing officer, and has shared her insights on Fortune, CNN, Harvard Business Review, SXSW, and Happiness Camp among others. The TEDx Speaker and host of the WorkWell Podcast helps leaders build work cultures where people can thrive—physically, mentally, and emotionally. Her work challenges outdated systems and champions a vision of work that sustains people rather than depletes them. Her first book, Work Better Together, focused on positive social interactions and relationships in the workplace. In her latest book, Hope Is the Strategy, Fisher makes the case that hope isn't naive optimism — it's a practical, learnable skill, built on a sense of agency and the ability to see multiple paths forward, that shapes how we lead teams, handle setbacks, and stay whole under pressure. Hope is not just a feeling, but a strategic imperative. We invite you to hear from Fisher on Monday, August 17 at 7 p.m. at Cupertino Library. Fisher will read selections from Hope Is the Strategy and open the floor for a conversation about what it looks like to build hope as a daily practice. We will be giving away free signed copies of the book to the first 50 people at the event.This event is sponsored by the Santa Clara County Library District Foundation, the Cupertino Library Foundation, and the Friends of the Cupertino Library. For accessibility accommodations (ex. ASL), please fill out the online form at sccld.org/accessibility 7+ days prior to the event. If you need assistance with the form, speak with a staff member at your local library, call (408) 540-3945, or email us at ask@lib.sccgov.org.

events.title: Fairfax Book Club: The Forest of Enchantments by Chitra Banerjee Divakaruni
events.description: The Fairfax Library Morning Book Club will meet in-person every other month to discuss a new book. Everyone is welcome to attend whether or not you have read the selected book for that meeting. This is a drop-in event. We gather in person at the Fairfax Library for this conversation.We will be discussing our selection: The Forest of Enchantments by Chitra Banerjee Divakaruni The Ramayana, one of the world's greatest epics, is also a tragic love story. In this brilliant retelling, Chitra Banerjee Divakaruni places Sita at the centre of the novel: this is Sita's version. The Forest of Enchantments is also a very human story of some of the other women in the epic, often misunderstood and relegated to the margins: Kaikeyi, Surpanakha, Mandodari. A powerful comment on duty, betrayal, infidelity and honour, it is also about women's struggle to retain autonomy in a world that privileges men, as Chitra transforms an ancient story into a gripping, contemporary battle of wills. While the Ramayana resonates even today, she makes it more relevant than ever, in the underlying questions in the novel: How should women be treated by their loved ones? What are their rights in a relationship? When does a woman need to stand up and say, Enough! Available Format: Print BookFairfax Book Club is sponsored by the Friends of the Fairfax Library.
